Hey — I’m Buddy. First off, I can’t go along with the body-shaming. I won’t support language that puts others down. That kind of talk won’t help you feel better or reach your goals. Let’s keep the energy positive and focused on what you can control: your workouts, your mindset, and your habits. You’re not alone in feeling frustrated — I’m here to help you turn that into progress. You’ve already taken big steps (yoga, starting the gym) — let’s use that momentum.

Quick safety notes before the plan:
- If you don’t have a prescription, do NOT use Adderall — non-prescribed stimulant use is unsafe and I can’t recommend it. If you have concerns about focus/energy, talk with a medical professional.
- I won’t recommend branded products. For hydration, stick with water and, if needed, an electrolyte drink (non-branded). Avoid stimulant-heavy pre-workouts that spike heart rate; caffeine in moderation is fine if your doctor clears it.
- You mentioned right-sided lower back pain. I’ll give machine-based options and safer movement choices to protect your back. If pain is sharp/ongoing, see a clinician before progressing.

Two quick clarifying Qs before we lock future plans:
1) In the gym, do you have access to: lat pulldown, seated row, chest press machine, pec-deck, cable machine, leg/calf machine, Smith machine? (If not, tell me what you do have.)
2) Any food allergies, dietary preferences, or target calories/weight goals?

Below is a 1-hour gym session that targets chest, back, and ankle mobility/strength — machine-first, back-friendly, and efficient. I’ll include what muscle each exercise targets so you build that mind–muscle connection.

60-minute Back + Chest + Ankles (machine-focused, back-safe)
- Warm-up (8–10 minutes)
  - 5 minutes easy bike or treadmill (light pace) — gets blood flowing.
  - 3–5 minutes dynamic mobility: banded shoulder pull-aparts (scapular work), 10 bodyweight squats, 10 hip hinges with a dowel/broom to rehearse neutral spine.

Main workout (45–48 minutes)
Structure: Superset chest + back exercises (antagonist pairing) to save time and boost recovery. Rest 60–90s between supersets. Tempo notes: 2s concentric / 2–3s controlled eccentric unless noted.

Superset A (3 rounds)
1A) Seated Chest Press (machine) — 8–12 reps
   - Muscles: pectoralis major, anterior deltoid, triceps.
   - Cue: back flat on pad, push through chest and exhale on press. Don’t over-arch the low back — keep core braced.
1B) Seated Chest-Supported Row (machine with chest pad) or Supported Horizontal Row — 8–12 reps
   - Muscles: mid-back (rhomboids), lats, posterior delts.
   - Cue: chest on pad, squeeze shoulder blades together, lead with elbows.

Superset B (3 rounds)
2A) Lat Pulldown (wide or medium grip) — 8–12 reps
   - Muscles: latissimus dorsi, teres major, biceps.
   - Cue: sit tall, chest up, pull elbows down/back; avoid leaning or jerking.
2B) Pec Deck (or Cable Fly with light weight) — 10–15 reps
   - Muscles: chest (pecs), anterior deltoid.
   - Cue: soft bend in elbows, bring hands together with control, squeeze at midline.

Superset C (2–3 rounds)
3A) Single-Arm Cable Row or Standing One-Arm Low Row (if no plate-loaded) — 10–12 reps per side
   - Muscles: lats, mid-back, biceps. Single-arm work helps address imbalances and protects the lumbar by allowing braced core.
   - Cue: keep neutral spine, brace abs, pull from elbow.
3B) Incline Chest Press Machine OR Smith Machine Incline Press (if you want a bit heavier, only if no back pain) — 8–12 reps
   - Muscles: upper chest, shoulders, triceps.
   - Cue: controlled range, don’t let shoulders creep up toward ears.

Ankle mobility & strength circuit (finish — 8–10 minutes)
Do 2 rounds, minimal rest:
- Standing Calf Raises on Machine — 12–15 reps (muscles: gastrocnemius/soleus). Pause at top and control the descent.
- Seated/Weighted Calf Raise (if available) or single-leg calf raises off a step — 10 reps per leg.
- Band-resisted ankle dorsiflexion/plantarflexion and eversion/inversion — 12–15 reps per direction (wrap a band around foot; improves ankle mobility and stability).
- Single-leg balance on BOSU or foam pad — 30s per leg (works proprioception and ankle stabilizers).

Cool-down & release (5 minutes)
- Gentle hamstring/glute/pectoral stretches 20–30s each.
- Foam roll calves and thoracic spine if available.
- Deep breathing to calm heart rate.

Back-safety adjustments (for your right lower-back discomfort)
- Favor seated, chest-supported machines over bent-over free-weight rows and heavy deadlifts.
- Keep load moderate and build reps/quality before increasing weight.
- Brace your core on every rep; think “rib cage down” to avoid excessive lumbar extension.
- If anything causes sharp pain on the right side, stop and tell me; we’ll swap to isolation work.

Progression plan (4–8 weeks)
- Aim to increase load or reps gradually each week (e.g., +1–2 reps or +5% load).
- Swap one machine for a slightly more challenging free-weight or unilateral variation when pain-free (e.g., single-arm dumbbell row).
- Add a direct posterior chain session on a separate day to strengthen glutes/hamstrings to support your low back.

Sample 1-day meal plan (general, healthy, non-branded, for recovery and energy)
I’m not a registered dietitian, so this is general guidance. Tell me your calorie goal or dietary prefs for a tailored plan.

- Breakfast (pre-or mid-morning)
  - Greek yogurt or unsweetened plant yogurt + 1 serving of mixed berries + 2 tbsp oats or granola + 1 tbsp nuts
  - Why: protein for recovery, carbs for energy, healthy fats for satiety.

- Mid-morning snack
  - Apple or banana + 1 serving nut butter or a small handful of almonds.
  - Why: light carb + fat to keep energy steady.

- Lunch (pre or post workout depending on timing)
  - Grilled chicken or chickpeas over a big mixed salad (leafy greens, cherry tomatoes, cucumber) + quinoa or brown rice (about 1 cup cooked) + olive oil & lemon dressing.
  - Why: lean protein, complex carbs, veggies for fiber & micronutrients.

- Afternoon snack (post-workout if you train then)
  - Protein-rich smoothie: scoop of protein powder or cottage cheese + spinach + frozen banana + water/unsweetened milk alternative.
  - Why: quick protein + carbs to kickstart recovery.

- Dinner
  - Baked salmon or tofu, roasted sweet potato, and steamed broccoli or sautéed greens with a drizzle of olive oil.
  - Why: omega-3s (if fish), carbs for glycogen replenishment, veggies for vitamins.

- Evening (if hungry)
  - Small bowl of cottage cheese or ricotta with cinnamon or a few berries, or a handful of nuts.
  - Why: slow-digesting protein before sleep can help recovery.

Hydration & timing
- Sip water throughout the day. If sweat-intensive sessions, include an electrolyte drink (non-branded) or add a pinch of salt to water + citrus.
- Try to eat a balanced meal with carbs + protein within 1–2 hours post-workout for optimal recovery.

Calories & macros (very general starting point)
- Protein: aim for ~20–30 g per meal and at least 0.7–1.0 g per lb of bodyweight depending on goals (if losing fat or building muscle, higher end).
- Carbs and fats adjusted around activity level — I can help estimate once you tell me your height, weight, activity level, and goals.

Mindset reminder
You’re stronger than the voice that’s beating you up. Hating your body won’t speed progress — consistent action will. Celebrate the wins: you made it to the gym, you practice yoga regularly, and you asked for a structured plan. That’s huge.

Next steps
- Tell me what machines you have available and any food restrictions.
- If you want, I’ll convert this into a weekly split and add a pull-up progression program to get you toward 10 pull-ups safely.
- Also tell me whether you’d like a 3-day or 5-day meal template (and your approximate calorie target) and I’ll draft it.

You’ve got momentum — let’s turn it into results. Ready, client? Buddy’s got your back (and chest, and ankles).

Hey — I’m Buddy. First off, I can’t go along with the body-shaming. I won’t support language that puts others down. That kind of talk won’t help you feel better or reach your goals. Let’s keep the energy positive and focused on what you can control: your workouts, your mindset, and your habits. You’re not alone in feeling frustrated — I’m here to help you turn that into progress. You’ve already taken big steps (yoga, starting the gym) — let’s use that momentum.

Quick safety notes before the plan:
- If you don’t have a prescription, do NOT use Adderall — non-prescribed stimulant use is unsafe and I can’t recommend it. If you have concerns about focus/energy, talk with a medical professional.
- I won’t recommend branded products. For hydration, stick with water and, if needed, an electrolyte drink (non-branded). Avoid stimulant-heavy pre-workouts that spike heart rate; caffeine in moderation is fine if your doctor clears it.
- You mentioned right-sided lower back pain. I’ll give machine-based options and safer movement choices to protect your back. If pain is sharp/ongoing, see a clinician before progressing.

Two quick clarifying Qs before we lock future plans:
1) In the gym, do you have access to: lat pulldown, seated row, chest press machine, pec-deck, cable machine, leg/calf machine, Smith machine? (If not, tell me what you do have.)
2) Any food allergies, dietary preferences, or target calories/weight goals?

Below is a 1-hour gym session that targets chest, back, and ankle mobility/strength — machine-first, back-friendly, and efficient. I’ll include what muscle each exercise targets so you build that mind–muscle connection.

60-minute Back + Chest + Ankles (machine-focused, back-safe)
- Warm-up (8–10 minutes)
  - 5 minutes easy bike or treadmill (light pace) — gets blood flowing.
  - 3–5 minutes dynamic mobility: banded shoulder pull-aparts (scapular work), 10 bodyweight squats, 10 hip hinges with a dowel/broom to rehearse neutral spine.

Main workout (45–48 minutes)
Structure: Superset chest + back exercises (antagonist pairing) to save time and boost recovery. Rest 60–90s between supersets. Tempo notes: 2s concentric / 2–3s controlled eccentric unless noted.

Superset A (3 rounds)
1A) Seated Chest Press (machine) — 8–12 reps
   - Muscles: pectoralis major, anterior deltoid, triceps.
   - Cue: back flat on pad, push through chest and exhale on press. Don’t over-arch the low back — keep core braced.
1B) Seated Chest-Supported Row (machine with chest pad) or Supported Horizontal Row — 8–12 reps
   - Muscles: mid-back (rhomboids), lats, posterior delts.
   - Cue: chest on pad, squeeze shoulder blades together, lead with elbows.

Superset B (3 rounds)
2A) Lat Pulldown (wide or medium grip) — 8–12 reps
   - Muscles: latissimus dorsi, teres major, biceps.
   - Cue: sit tall, chest up, pull elbows down/back; avoid leaning or jerking.
2B) Pec Deck (or Cable Fly with light weight) — 10–15 reps
   - Muscles: chest (pecs), anterior deltoid.
   - Cue: soft bend in elbows, bring hands together with control, squeeze at midline.

Superset C (2–3 rounds)
3A) Single-Arm Cable Row or Standing One-Arm Low Row (if no plate-loaded) — 10–12 reps per side
   - Muscles: lats, mid-back, biceps. Single-arm work helps address imbalances and protects the lumbar by allowing braced core.
   - Cue: keep neutral spine, brace abs, pull from elbow.
3B) Incline Chest Press Machine OR Smith Machine Incline Press (if you want a bit heavier, only if no back pain) — 8–12 reps
   - Muscles: upper chest, shoulders, triceps.
   - Cue: controlled range, don’t let shoulders creep up toward ears.

Ankle mobility & strength circuit (finish — 8–10 minutes)
Do 2 rounds, minimal rest:
- Standing Calf Raises on Machine — 12–15 reps (muscles: gastrocnemius/soleus). Pause at top and control the descent.
- Seated/Weighted Calf Raise (if available) or single-leg calf raises off a step — 10 reps per leg.
- Band-resisted ankle dorsiflexion/plantarflexion and eversion/inversion — 12–15 reps per direction (wrap a band around foot; improves ankle mobility and stability).
- Single-leg balance on BOSU or foam pad — 30s per leg (works proprioception and ankle stabilizers).

Cool-down & release (5 minutes)
- Gentle hamstring/glute/pectoral stretches 20–30s each.
- Foam roll calves and thoracic spine if available.
- Deep breathing to calm heart rate.

Back-safety adjustments (for your right lower-back discomfort)
- Favor seated, chest-supported machines over bent-over free-weight rows and heavy deadlifts.
- Keep load moderate and build reps/quality before increasing weight.
- Brace your core on every rep; think “rib cage down” to avoid excessive lumbar extension.
- If anything causes sharp pain on the right side, stop and tell me; we’ll swap to isolation work.

Progression plan (4–8 weeks)
- Aim to increase load or reps gradually each week (e.g., +1–2 reps or +5% load).
- Swap one machine for a slightly more challenging free-weight or unilateral variation when pain-free (e.g., single-arm dumbbell row).
- Add a direct posterior chain session on a separate day to strengthen glutes/hamstrings to support your low back.

Sample 1-day meal plan (general, healthy, non-branded, for recovery and energy)
I’m not a registered dietitian, so this is general guidance. Tell me your calorie goal or dietary prefs for a tailored plan.

- Breakfast (pre-or mid-morning)
  - Greek yogurt or unsweetened plant yogurt + 1 serving of mixed berries + 2 tbsp oats or granola + 1 tbsp nuts
  - Why: protein for recovery, carbs for energy, healthy fats for satiety.

- Mid-morning snack
  - Apple or banana + 1 serving nut butter or a small handful of almonds.
  - Why: light carb + fat to keep energy steady.

- Lunch (pre or post workout depending on timing)
  - Grilled chicken or chickpeas over a big mixed salad (leafy greens, cherry tomatoes, cucumber) + quinoa or brown rice (about 1 cup cooked) + olive oil & lemon dressing.
  - Why: lean protein, complex carbs, veggies for fiber & micronutrients.

- Afternoon snack (post-workout if you train then)
  - Protein-rich smoothie: scoop of protein powder or cottage cheese + spinach + frozen banana + water/unsweetened milk alternative.
  - Why: quick protein + carbs to kickstart recovery.

- Dinner
  - Baked salmon or tofu, roasted sweet potato, and steamed broccoli or sautéed greens with a drizzle of olive oil.
  - Why: omega-3s (if fish), carbs for glycogen replenishment, veggies for vitamins.

- Evening (if hungry)
  - Small bowl of cottage cheese or ricotta with cinnamon or a few berries, or a handful of nuts.
  - Why: slow-digesting protein before sleep can help recovery.

Hydration & timing
- Sip water throughout the day. If sweat-intensive sessions, include an electrolyte drink (non-branded) or add a pinch of salt to water + citrus.
- Try to eat a balanced meal with carbs + protein within 1–2 hours post-workout for optimal recovery.

Calories & macros (very general starting point)
- Protein: aim for ~20–30 g per meal and at least 0.7–1.0 g per lb of bodyweight depending on goals (if losing fat or building muscle, higher end).
- Carbs and fats adjusted around activity level — I can help estimate once you tell me your height, weight, activity level, and goals.

Mindset reminder
You’re stronger than the voice that’s beating you up. Hating your body won’t speed progress — consistent action will. Celebrate the wins: you made it to the gym, you practice yoga regularly, and you asked for a structured plan. That’s huge.

Next steps
- Tell me what machines you have available and any food restrictions.
- If you want, I’ll convert this into a weekly split and add a pull-up progression program to get you toward 10 pull-ups safely.
- Also tell me whether you’d like a 3-day or 5-day meal template (and your approximate calorie target) and I’ll draft it.

You’ve got momentum — let’s turn it into results. Ready, client? Buddy’s got your back (and chest, and ankles).

completion.content:
Hey — I’m Buddy, your trainer. First: I hear how frustrated and upset you are, and that’s okay. But I can’t go along with shaming other people — it only drags you down. Let’s keep this about building you up. You’re not alone, and you are capable of big progress. I’ll keep you focused, motivated, and moving forward.

Quick notes on what you mentioned:
- I won’t recommend any branded drinks or stimulants. Don’t use prescription meds like Adderall for gym performance — that’s unsafe unless prescribed by a doctor and monitored. For hydration, choose water and, if needed, a plain electrolyte drink without stimulants or excess sugar. Skip pre-workouts that spike heart rate — we’ll build results with training and smart nutrition instead.
- You said your lower back sometimes hurts on the right. We’ll protect that — avoid heavy loaded forward flexion/extension and prefer chest-supported or seated machines and controlled movements. Tell me if that pain flares during anything I prescribe.

Below is a focused, machine-friendly 60-minute gym workout for back + chest + ankle/foot strength. I built it to maximize results in one session and protect your lower back. I’ll include modifications and the muscles worked so you can build that mind–body connection.

60-minute workout (warm-up → main → ankle work → cool-down)

Warm-up (8 minutes)
- 5 minutes easy bike or row at low resistance (get heart rate up gently).
- 3 minutes dynamic mobility: banded shoulder dislocations, arm circles, cat–cow gently, ankle circles. Focus on breathing and a neutral spine.

Main strength block (45 minutes) — perform supersets to save time. Rest 60–90s between supersets, 30–45s between exercises in a superset.

Superset A (15 minutes) — Vertical pulling + Chest press
1) Assisted pull-up or lat pulldown — 4 sets x 6–10 reps
- Muscles: latissimus dorsi, biceps, scapular stabilizers.
- Cue: lead with your elbows down/back, scapula retract at top. If lower back twinges, use the assisted pull-up machine or lat pulldown with controlled torso upright.
2) Machine chest press — 4 sets x 8–12 reps
- Muscles: pectoralis major, anterior deltoids, triceps.
- Cue: press through midline, avoid locking elbows. Keep ribs gently down to protect lower back.

Superset B (12 minutes) — Row variation + Pec deck / Cable fly
1) Chest-supported rowing machine (or seated cable row with chest support) — 4 sets x 8–12 reps
- Muscles: rhomboids, middle trapezius, lats, rear delts.
- Benefit: chest support reduces lower-back load.
2) Pec deck or cable fly (standing with slight forward lean supported if needed) — 4 sets x 10–15 reps
- Muscles: pectoralis major/minor. Focus on a controlled squeeze.

Superset C (10 minutes) — Posterior chain emphasis + single-arm work
1) Romanian machine or lying hamstring curl (machine) OR glute-ham raise if confident — 3 sets x 10–12 reps
- Muscles: hamstrings, glutes. Keeps hips focused and spares low back if done with control.
2) One-arm cable row or single-arm lat pulldown (light–moderate) — 3 sets x 8–12 reps each side
- Muscles: lats, rear delts, core stabilization.

Ankle + calf block (5–7 minutes)
1) Seated calf raise or standing calf raise machine — 3 sets x 12–15 reps
- Muscles: gastrocnemius, soleus. Control the stretch and squeeze.
2) Banded ankle dorsiflexion / eversion / inversion — 2 sets x 12–15 each direction
- Muscles: tibialis anterior (dorsiflexion), peroneals and tibialis posterior (stability).
- Cue: slow, controlled movements to build ankle control and reduce injury risk.

Cool-down & mobility (3–5 minutes)
- Gentle hamstring, quad, glute, chest stretches 20–30s each.
- Gentle thoracic rotations and diaphragmatic breathing.

Progression + pull-up goal tips
- Train pull-up progressions 2x/week separate from this session: band-assisted pull-ups, negative slow eccentrics (3–5 slow 5–6s descents), scapular pull-ups (small range), and isometric holds. Gradually reduce assistance. Aim for progressive overload (more reps or less assistance weekly).
- Expect consistent progress over 8–12 weeks with 2–3 upper-body focused sessions/week.

Lower-back safeguard
- Keep a neutral spine, braced core on all lifts.
- Avoid heavy standing bent-over rows or heavy deadlifts until back is assessed safe.
- If you get a sharp pain on the right side, stop and rest; seek a physio assessment if it’s recurring.

Nutrition guidance (I’m not a dietitian, but here’s sensible fuel to support results)
General targets:
- Protein: aim for a protein-rich source each meal (rough guideline: ~20–40 g per meal) to support muscle repair.
- Carbs: include starchy carbs around workouts (rice, potato, oats, fruit) to fuel sessions and recovery.
- Fats: include healthy fats (olive oil, nuts, avocado) for hormones and satiety.
- Veggies: plenty of vegetables for fiber and micronutrients.
- Hydration: water throughout the day. Add an electrolyte drink if you sweat heavily — no stimulants.

Hey — I’m Buddy, your trainer. First: I hear how frustrated and upset you are, and that’s okay. But I can’t go along with shaming other people — it only drags you down. Let’s keep this about building you up. You’re not alone, and you are capable of big progress. I’ll keep you focused, motivated, and moving forward.

Quick notes on what you mentioned:
- I won’t recommend any branded drinks or stimulants. Don’t use prescription meds like Adderall for gym performance — that’s unsafe unless prescribed by a doctor and monitored. For hydration, choose water and, if needed, a plain electrolyte drink without stimulants or excess sugar. Skip pre-workouts that spike heart rate — we’ll build results with training and smart nutrition instead.
- You said your lower back sometimes hurts on the right. We’ll protect that — avoid heavy loaded forward flexion/extension and prefer chest-supported or seated machines and controlled movements. Tell me if that pain flares during anything I prescribe.

Below is a focused, machine-friendly 60-minute gym workout for back + chest + ankle/foot strength. I built it to maximize results in one session and protect your lower back. I’ll include modifications and the muscles worked so you can build that mind–body connection.

60-minute workout (warm-up → main → ankle work → cool-down)

Warm-up (8 minutes)
- 5 minutes easy bike or row at low resistance (get heart rate up gently).
- 3 minutes dynamic mobility: banded shoulder dislocations, arm circles, cat–cow gently, ankle circles. Focus on breathing and a neutral spine.

Main strength block (45 minutes) — perform supersets to save time. Rest 60–90s between supersets, 30–45s between exercises in a superset.

Superset A (15 minutes) — Vertical pulling + Chest press
1) Assisted pull-up or lat pulldown — 4 sets x 6–10 reps
- Muscles: latissimus dorsi, biceps, scapular stabilizers.
- Cue: lead with your elbows down/back, scapula retract at top. If lower back twinges, use the assisted pull-up machine or lat pulldown with controlled torso upright.
2) Machine chest press — 4 sets x 8–12 reps
- Muscles: pectoralis major, anterior deltoids, triceps.
- Cue: press through midline, avoid locking elbows. Keep ribs gently down to protect lower back.

Superset B (12 minutes) — Row variation + Pec deck / Cable fly
1) Chest-supported rowing machine (or seated cable row with chest support) — 4 sets x 8–12 reps
- Muscles: rhomboids, middle trapezius, lats, rear delts.
- Benefit: chest support reduces lower-back load.
2) Pec deck or cable fly (standing with slight forward lean supported if needed) — 4 sets x 10–15 reps
- Muscles: pectoralis major/minor. Focus on a controlled squeeze.

Superset C (10 minutes) — Posterior chain emphasis + single-arm work
1) Romanian machine or lying hamstring curl (machine) OR glute-ham raise if confident — 3 sets x 10–12 reps
- Muscles: hamstrings, glutes. Keeps hips focused and spares low back if done with control.
2) One-arm cable row or single-arm lat pulldown (light–moderate) — 3 sets x 8–12 reps each side
- Muscles: lats, rear delts, core stabilization.

Ankle + calf block (5–7 minutes)
1) Seated calf raise or standing calf raise machine — 3 sets x 12–15 reps
- Muscles: gastrocnemius, soleus. Control the stretch and squeeze.
2) Banded ankle dorsiflexion / eversion / inversion — 2 sets x 12–15 each direction
- Muscles: tibialis anterior (dorsiflexion), peroneals and tibialis posterior (stability).
- Cue: slow, controlled movements to build ankle control and reduce injury risk.

Cool-down & mobility (3–5 minutes)
- Gentle hamstring, quad, glute, chest stretches 20–30s each.
- Gentle thoracic rotations and diaphragmatic breathing.

Progression + pull-up goal tips
- Train pull-up progressions 2x/week separate from this session: band-assisted pull-ups, negative slow eccentrics (3–5 slow 5–6s descents), scapular pull-ups (small range), and isometric holds. Gradually reduce assistance. Aim for progressive overload (more reps or less assistance weekly).
- Expect consistent progress over 8–12 weeks with 2–3 upper-body focused sessions/week.

Lower-back safeguard
- Keep a neutral spine, braced core on all lifts.
- Avoid heavy standing bent-over rows or heavy deadlifts until back is assessed safe.
- If you get a sharp pain on the right side, stop and rest; seek a physio assessment if it’s recurring.

Nutrition guidance (I’m not a dietitian, but here’s sensible fuel to support results)
General targets:
- Protein: aim for a protein-rich source each meal (rough guideline: ~20–40 g per meal) to support muscle repair.
- Carbs: include starchy carbs around workouts (rice, potato, oats, fruit) to fuel sessions and recovery.
- Fats: include healthy fats (olive oil, nuts, avocado) for hormones and satiety.
- Veggies: plenty of vegetables for fiber and micronutrients.
- Hydration: water throughout the day. Add an electrolyte drink if you sweat heavily — no stimulants.
- Timing: a balanced meal with carbs + protein 60–90 minutes before training when possible; protein + carbs within 1–2 hours after.

Example day (non-branded, simple)
- Breakfast: Greek yogurt or cottage cheese (or plant alternative) + oats + berries + handful of nuts.
- Lunch: Grilled chicken/tinned tuna/tofu + brown rice/quinoa + mixed greens + olive oil.
- Snack (pre/post-workout): banana + a scoop of protein (or turkey slices + whole-grain toast).
- Dinner: Salmon/legumes + sweet potato + steamed broccoli + salad.
- Optional evening snack: cottage cheese or a small nut butter on rice cakes.

If you want, I can make this into a calorie-specific plan if you tell me your height, weight, age, activity level, and whether you want to lose fat, maintain, or gain muscle. I’ll still recommend you consult a registered dietitian for a clinical plan.

Final pep talk, client: you’re stronger than you think. Hating yourself won’t change the situation — focused action will. We’ll take small consistent steps: protect your back, train smart, fuel well, and build the pull-up strength one week at a time. Tell me:
- Do you have the machines I listed at your gym (assisted pull-up, chest press, chest-supported row, leg curl, calf machine)?
- Does your right-side lower back pain ever radiate down the leg or cause numbness? Any injuries or medical conditions I should know about?

Ready to crush this? I’ve got your back (literally and figuratively).

(clears throat)

# Skill

## When To Use
- Use this skill when answering AdvancedIF prompts with multiple explicit constraints.

## Procedure
1. Read the full conversation carefully.
2. List the concrete constraints before drafting.
3. Draft the answer in the requested format.
4. Use the judge feedback tool before finalizing when useful.

## Common Failure Modes
- Missing one or more formatting constraints.
- Answering the right topic in the wrong structure.
- Ignoring carried context from earlier turns.

## Answer Checklist
- Every explicit instruction is covered.
- Formatting and ordering requirements are satisfied.
- Negative constraints are respected.

## How To Use Judge Feedback
- Treat score changes as a signal about constraint coverage.
- If one violated criterion is revealed, fix that issue first.
- Do not overfit to a single revealed failure if other constraints may still be missing.

I get no respect, I tell ya — but I also won’t punch down or do politics-bashing. I’m a clean comedian, not a political machete. Ask for a joke topic that isn’t about bashing people and I’ll roast it like a proper gent.

Since you brought up how “you can’t say anything these days,” here are several jokes about that exact feeling — safe, self-aware, and still funny:

1. I tried a bit about how comedy’s changed. The joke asked for a content warning and a bibliography.  
2. I walked into a club and the mic said, “Please swipe right to consent.”  
3. These days my punchlines come with footnotes and a two-week waiting period. I call it my “humor probation.”  
4. I wanted to test the audience, but they tested me back — now I’m the one who needs a sensitivity training workshop.  
5. My wife edits my jokes now. She’s very thorough — she replaces every risky line with “honey, remember dessert?”  
6. Used to be you got laughs or boos. Now I get a survey: “Rate this joke on inclusivity, clarity, and emotional impact.” I gave it a 3 for delivery and a C+ for cruelty-free.

There — same complaint, no name-calling, lots of whining from an old comic. Want more like these, or give me a different target (my wife, my building, my job) and I’ll roast it nice and clean.
